races.name: Tour de Romandie – Prologue
races.finish_type: Uphill time trial finish (800m ramp @ 6.9% avg)
races.description: The 79th Tour de Romandie opens with a 3.2km prologue in Villars-sur-Glâne, a suburb of Fribourg. The course begins from Route de Moncor, descends steeply in the first two kilometres, then swings through a wide arc before the road tilts sharply uphill for the final 1.5km — including an 800m ramp at 6.9% average gradient where all the significant time differences will be made. There are few sharp corners; the profile heavily favours powerful climber-time-triallist hybrids rather than pure aerodynamic specialists. Elapsed time is expected to be around 3.5–4 minutes for the fastest riders. The prologue is notable for being Tadej Pogačar's first stage race appearance of 2026 — he is racing a stage race later in his career than in any previous season, arriving direct from his fourth Liège-Bastogne-Liège victory just 48 hours ago. Given the climbing nature of the Romandie prologue, the usual prologue specialists are less dominant here than at a flat course like the Tour de France. Weather forecast: heavy rain with possible thunderstorms, 16°C, little wind.

stage_race_context.notes: Only one summit finish in the entire race (Stage 5 to Leysin). Deliberately designed as a climbers' Romandie with no second time trial. Pogačar's stage race debut for 2026 — never previously started Tour de Romandie. Race director confirmed Pogačar was not paid to attend ('Pogačar is not at the Tour de Romandie for money').

stages.name: Prologue
stages.notes: TODAY. Uphill finish. Rain forecast. First yellow jersey.

stages.type: Circuit with uphill, then downhill finish
stages.notes: Côte de la Rasse x3 (2.2km @ 8.9%), then Cat.1 Ovronnaz (8.9km @ 9.7%). Summit 33.8km from finish — long descent to Martigny. GC riders test.
Looped circuit around rolling Vaud terrain. Côte de Vuilliens (3.6km @ 4.8%) is the circuit climb, final ascent crested inside 5km. Puncheurs' day.
Multiple laps with Col de Mollendruz (9km @ 6.1%) on final lap. 21.4km descent then flat run-in to Orbe.
Queen stage by elevation. Jaun Pass (Jaunpass, 8.1km @ 8.3%) tackled twice from different sides plus Saanenmöser. 16.7km descent to Charmey finish.
ONLY summit finish. Leysin: 13.9km @ 5.9% avg, max 12%. Final showdown for yellow. Described as 'reminiscent of Liège-Bastogne-Liège but with a summit finish.'

races.rider_notes: Prologue top favourites: Ivo Oliveira (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) is the most likely winner — the Portuguese rider was 2nd at this prologue in 2025 and is a genuine TT specialist who also climbs well (suiting the uphill finish). Ethan Hayter (Soudal Quick-Step) is a past Romandie prologue winner and an explosive puncheur with strong TT ability. Tadej Pogačar (UAE) is categorised as an 'outsider' for the prologue itself by IDL — at 3.2km, the uphill section plays to him, but pure TT men are usually faster over such short distances. He is the overwhelming GC favourite however, debuting at Romandie direct from 4 race days in 2026 (all wins except Paris-Roubaix 2nd). Primož Roglič (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe) is also a strong prologue performer when fresh. Florian Lipowitz (Red Bull) and Oscar Onley (INEOS) lead the GC pack. Luke Plapp (Jayco AlUla), Rémi Cavagna (FDJ), and Mauro Schmid (Jayco, fresh from Flèche 2nd place) add sprint TT power. Notable absentees from start list: Lidl-Trek, Uno-X, Decathlon CMA CGM. 14 WorldTeams + Tudor Pro Cycling. 15 teams total, 105 riders.

gc_contenders.notes: Romandie debut. 4 wins from 5 race days in 2026. Won LBL 48h ago. Last stage race: Tour de France 2025.
2-time Romandie winner (2018, 2019). Back together with teammate Lipowitz for first time this season.
Strongest Red Bull performer so far in 2026. Won Itzulia Basque Country stage. Alongside Roglič.
Scottish climber, looked strong at Volta ao Algarve before illness at Paris-Nice. First serious test.
2nd in 2025 Romandie overall. Strong spring — 2nd Volta a Catalunya, 4th Flèche Wallonne.
2nd Paris-Nice. Red Bull's third GC card.
Skipping Giro this year, targeting stage races. Back racing after Itzulia DNF.

broadcasts.notes: Live coverage split across TNT Sports channels (prologue on TNT Sports main). Broadcast from ~16:00 CEST (14:00 UTC). From £25.99/month standalone or £30.99/month month-to-month.
FloBikes. $39.99/month or $155.88/year. NOT on Peacock or Max.
RTS is the Swiss public broadcaster — free live coverage in Switzerland.
Free on L'Équipe TV in France.
Eurosport 1 with English commentary across European markets, via discovery+ or HBO Max depending on country.

No Australian broadcaster has picked up Tour de Romandie 2026 rights. Cyclingnews explicitly confirms 'no Australian channels have picked up Tour de Romandie.' Australian viewers need a VPN to access their usual European stream (e.g. RTS via Swiss VPN server).

data_confidence.notes: Broadcast details corrected. Cyclingnews 2026 how-to-watch guide confirms: UK = TNT Sports/HBO Max; US/CA = FloBikes only (NOT Peacock or Max); Switzerland = RTS free; France = L'Equipe free; Europe = Eurosport/discovery+/HBO Max. Australia = NO COVERAGE — Cyclingnews explicitly states 'no Australian channels have picked up Tour de Romandie.' This is consistent with 2025 Cyclingnews guide which also stated no SBS coverage. The cycling archives preview article's mention of SBS was erroneous. Race distance: 3.2km per PCS. Start time 15:28 CEST per PCS.

races.name: Presidential Cycling Tour of Türkiye – Stage 3
races.description: Stage 3 of the Tour of Türkiye runs 134km from Marmaris along the Turkish Riviera coast to Kiran. After two consecutive sprint wins for Tom Crabbe (Team Flanders-Baloise, Belgian 20-year-old), this shorter and hillier stage along the Aegean/Mediterranean coast is expected to break up the pure sprinters and offer opportunities for the climbers and puncheurs. The Turkish Riviera terrain typically combines sea views with sharp, narrow climbs as the road winds up and down through coastal hills.

stages.result: Tom Crabbe (Team Flanders-Baloise) wins bunch sprint — 3rd win of 2026 season. Simon Dehairs 2nd, Davide Persico 3rd. Peloton split early.
Tom Crabbe wins again — 2nd consecutive stage. Dropped inside final 10km but fought back to win sprint.

stages.notes: TODAY. Shorter hilly stage — likely breaks sprint pattern.

recent_results.notes: Belgian 20-year-old wins messy sprint — 3rd win of 2026 season. Beats Simon Dehairs and Davide Persico. Described as a 'messy sprint.'
Crabbe dropped at 10km from finish but fought back to sprint to victory. Remarkable recovery ride.

broadcasts.notes: FloBikes has been streaming Tour of Türkiye stages LIVE on their official YouTube channel. Stage 1 and Stage 2 were both streamed free on YouTube. Check FloBikes YouTube channel on race day. The YouTube description says 'Watch every Tour of Turkey 2026 stage live, only on FloBikes' — but the actual stream is available on YouTube without a FloBikes subscription.
Full live coverage on FloBikes with commentary. $39.99/month.

Cybernews lists SBS as a free option in Australia for Tour of Türkiye, but this is NOT independently confirmed by Cyclingnews or SBS directly. Treat as uncertain — check SBS On Demand on race day. The YouTube stream (FloBikes channel) is a more reliable free option globally.
Cybernews lists TNT Sports as UK broadcaster, but this is not confirmed by Cyclingnews or Cycling Weekly. The free YouTube stream is a reliable alternative for UK viewers.
Domestic free coverage on TRT Sport (Turkish state broadcaster).

data_confidence.notes: Stage 3 Marmaris → Kiran, 134km confirmed by Cyclingnews stages page. Stage 1 winner Tom Crabbe (Team Flanders-Baloise) confirmed. Stage 2 winner Tom Crabbe confirmed. KEY BROADCAST UPDATE: Tour of Türkiye stages 1 and 2 were streamed LIVE on FloBikes' official YouTube channel (confirmed by YouTube URLs found in search results). This is the most accessible free stream globally. FloBikes (paid, full commentary) for US/CA confirmed. SBS Australia listed by cybernews but not independently confirmed — flagged as uncertain. TNT Sports UK listed by cybernews but not confirmed.

podium: 1. Tadej Pogačar (SLO, UAE Team Emirates-XRG) — 4th LBL title, solo
recent_results.notes: Epic edition. Evenepoel got into a 55-rider front group in the opening kilometres after an early crash split the peloton; Pogačar and Seixas chased from behind at up to 4 minutes. The gap was eroded over 160km as UAE (Tim Wellens, Benoît Cosnefroy, Pavel Sivakov, Domen Novak) drove the chase. On La Redoute (35km from finish), Cosnefroy launched a blistering lead-out and Pogačar attacked — only Seixas could follow. The 19-year-old then survived repeated attacks all the way to the Roche-aux-Faucons summit, but Pogačar's searing acceleration on the 11% climb finally broke him 13.9km from the finish. Pogačar solo'd home for a 4th consecutive LBL win. Seixas held 2nd (+45s), Evenepoel 3rd (+1:42). The average speed of 44.42 km/h is the fastest LBL in history, beating Pogačar's own 2025 record of 41.98 km/h. Pogačar honoured late teammate Cristian Camilo Muñoz (died that week) by touching his heart at the finish. His 13th Monument victory, joining Merckx (4 LBL wins). Pidcock had a mechanical with 113km to go and lost touch permanently.

podium: 1. Demi Vollering (NED, FDJ United-SUEZ) — 3rd LBL title, 35km solo from Redoute
4. Anna van der Breggen (NED, SD Worx-Protime) — raced despite illness
recent_results.notes: Vollering's record third Liège win, joining Van Vleuten (2 wins) whom she surpasses as the most successful women's LBL winner ever. Attacked on La Redoute with 34-35km to go and held off all chasers. Pieterse, Niewiadoma-Phinney and Van der Breggen chased together but couldn't close 1:28 deficit. Van der Breggen 4th despite racing ill. Femke Gerritse (SD Worx) went solo with 116km to go, built 2:20 lead before being caught before Stockeu. An aggressive race throughout. Vollering's spring 2026 is one for the history books: Omloop Het Nieuwsblad + Tour of Flanders + Flèche Wallonne + LBL Femmes.
Crabbe makes it 2/2 in Turkey — dropped inside final 10km but fought back to win the sprint anyway. Remarkable performance.

context.narrative: The spring classics season is over. Tadej Pogačar has completed the most dominant spring in modern cycling history: Strade Bianche, Milan-San Remo, Tour of Flanders, 2nd Paris-Roubaix, La Flèche Wallonne, and LBL — 5 wins and 6 top-2 finishes from just 6 race days. He begins his first stage race of 2026 today in Switzerland, just 48 hours after the fastest LBL ever. Demi Vollering has completed a historic Ardennes triple: Flèche Wallonne + LBL Femmes in one week, adding to Omloop and Flanders this spring — the most dominant women's spring since Anna van der Breggen's era. Paul Seixas (19yo) has announced himself as Pogačar's greatest rival in a generation, winning Itzulia + Flèche Wallonne and finishing 2nd at LBL in just his debut Ardennes week.

results: La Flèche Wallonne — 1st (Note: per Flèche file this is Seixas not Pogi — to be verified)
pogacar_2026_spring_record.notes: Cyclingnews says Pogačar had 'four wins from five race days, only miss being defeat at Paris-Roubaix.' Flèche Wallonne was won by Seixas, not Pogačar — the Cyclingnews article refers to his broader spring. His actual race count includes Strade, MSR, Flanders, Roubaix, LBL = 5 race days.

vollering_2026_spring_record.notes: Four Classics wins this spring. Historic.

upcoming.notes: Martigny circuit, 170.6km. Three ascents of Côte de la Rasse (2.2km @ 8.9%) then Cat.1 Ovronnaz (8.9km @ 9.7%). Summit 33.8km from finish — long descent to Martigny. First road test for Pogačar.
First Grand Tour of 2026. Starts in Rome. Key GC names: Jonas Vingegaard, Joao Almeida, Richard Carapaz, Filippo Ganna. Pogačar is not expected to ride the Giro — focusing on Tour de France.

issues: Tour de Romandie distance: PCS result page shows 3.2km; IDL and CyclingUpToDate previews say 3.1km; cyclingfantasy shows 3.17km; cyclingstage and domestique say 3.2km. Using 3.2km per PCS and most sources.

Tour de Romandie broadcast window: Cycling archives says 'prologue broadcast in full from 16:00 CET on April 28' — this is 16:00 CEST. Start time per PCS is 15:28 CEST (13:28 UTC). Broadcast begins ~32 minutes after first rider rolls out. UK broadcast via TNT Sports confirmed but exact BST timing not found — using 14:00 UTC (16:00 CEST) as broadcast start per cycling archives.
Tour of Türkiye Stage 3 start time: Not confirmed from search results. Using approximate 11:00 CEST based on Stage 1 being at 10:00 CEST and typical Turkish race timing.

BROADCAST CORRECTIONS: (1) Tour de Romandie: removed erroneous SBS Australia claim. Cyclingnews 2026 guide explicitly states 'no Australian channels have picked up Tour de Romandie.' Added L'Equipe France. (2) Tour of Türkiye: Added FloBikes YouTube channel as free global live stream — confirmed by YouTube search results showing Stage 1 and Stage 2 streamed on FloBikes YouTube. SBS Australia for Turkey now flagged as 'unverified' rather than confirmed.

Pogačar's Flèche Wallonne note in season context: Cyclingnews says 'four wins from five race days, only miss being defeat at Paris-Roubaix' — this counts Strade, MSR, Flanders, and LBL (4 wins), not Flèche which was won by Seixas. The cyclingnews quote is consistent with Pogačar not racing Flèche (he did race it per Cyclingnews LBL article saying 'back-to-back Mur de Huy' but this conflicts with the Flèche result showing Seixas as winner). Flagging for re-verification: the Cyclingnews LBL article about Pogačar says 'La Flèche Wallonne back-to-back' which would imply he won it — but our Flèche file clearly has Seixas as winner. This may be an error in the cycling archives preview article attributing Pogačar's 2025 AND 2026 Flèche — his 2025 Flèche win was confirmed; 2026 Flèche was won by Seixas.

No other 1.1/2.1+ races found for April 28 besides Tour de Romandie Prologue and Tour of Türkiye Stage 3. Cross-referenced against Cyclingnews calendar and Cyclingstage.com (latest addition April 26: Tour de Romandie route).
